name: PromptShield
description: Rule-based prompt-injection scanner plus runtime action inspector. A functional public prototype with a live Vercel demo — deterministic and heuristic by design, so false positives and negatives are possible.
keyFeatures: Deterministic regex/heuristic prompt scanner with six finding categories: instruction override, role hijack, jailbreak markers, system-prompt exfiltration, data exfiltration, unsafe tool use
Runtime inspection endpoint that classifies proposed agent actions as allow, block, or requires_approval
Authenticated dashboard with scan history and a bearer-authenticated tool-style HTTP API (scan_prompt, assess_risk)
Unit tests cover benign and malicious classification, false-positive regressions, and API shape
CI boots the production build, asserts real HTTP behavior (401 unauthenticated, benign=low, malicious=critical), and fails if the public demo is down
architecture:
graph TD
  User[Prompt or agent action] --> API[Bearer-auth HTTP API]
  API --> Scanner[Rule-based scanner - 6 categories]
  API --> Inspector[Runtime inspector - allow/block/requires_approval]
  Scanner --> DB[(Prisma/Postgres scan history)]
  DB --> Dash[Clerk-authenticated dashboard]
details: Live demo: https://promptshield-cyan.vercel.app. It is not a full MCP transport, and it does not use an LLM for detection — scanning is deliberately deterministic so results are reproducible. It is a showcase prototype, not a commercial product.

name: agentsec-hook-pack
description: Zero-dependency local PreToolUse policy hook for Claude Code and OpenAI Codex. A local hook package — not a hosted web application.
keyFeatures: Reads a tool-use event from stdin and returns Claude-style JSON decisions or Codex-style exit codes
Safe-command fast path with chained-command and find-exec bypass protection
Destructive-pattern blocking (rm -rf, force pushes, DROP TABLE and similar)
observe / prompt / enforce modes with fail-closed handling on malformed input or API outage
Optional remote policy call to a PromptShield-compatible endpoint — exercised against a mock server in tests, not a proven hosted approval platform
architecture:
sequenceDiagram
  participant Agent as AI Agent (Claude/Codex)
  participant Hook as agentsec-hook.mjs
  Agent->>Hook: PreToolUse event via stdin
  Hook->>Hook: safe-command fast path / destructive-pattern check
  alt optional remote policy configured
    Hook->>API: POST tool payload
    API-->>Hook: allow / deny / requires_approval
  end
  Hook-->>Agent: JSON decision (Claude) or exit code (Codex)
fileStructure: test/agentsec-hook.test.mjs - 12-case test suite incl. bypass attempts and API-outage fail-closed behavior
details: The 12-case node:test suite and CI cover bypass attempts, Codex exit-code semantics, and fail-closed behavior when the policy API is unreachable. Default mode is observe (reports without blocking).

name: mcpguard-lite
description: Static risk analyzer for raw MCP tools/list JSON manifests. Analyzes declared capabilities only — it does not probe running servers, and static manifest analysis does not prove runtime safety.
keyFeatures: Seven capability categories ranked by severity from declared tool manifests
Missing-control detection: approval gates, audit logging, sandboxing, egress limits
Distinguishes positive control declarations from negative wording like 'without approval'
Handles malformed JSON input gracefully
Unit-tested; CI runs vitest, typecheck, lint, build, and checks the live demo
architecture:
graph TD
  Paste[Pasted tools/list JSON] --> Analyzer[Deterministic static analyzer]
  Analyzer --> Risks[Capability risks by severity]
  Analyzer --> Gaps[Missing declared controls]
  Risks --> Dash[Scan dashboard + events]
details: Live demo: https://mcpguard-lite.vercel.app (may cold-start slowly). Built on a shared Next.js/Prisma/Clerk scaffold also used by agentmap and approveops.

name: agentmap
description: AI-agent inventory with explainable 0-100 risk scoring and readiness labels, computed from declared permissions, data access, and tool surface. Scoring is metadata-based reporting — it does not observe or enforce anything at runtime.
keyFeatures: Deterministic 0-100 risk score from declared metadata: broad permissions, command execution, secret access, and sensitive data raise the score
Readiness labels: ready / needs_review / blocked with threshold logic
Agent CRUD via /api/agents plus a dashboard with agents, MCP, security, and settings pages
Scoring logic unit-tested; CI exercises the API against the production build and checks the live demo
architecture:
graph TD
  Reg[Agent registration - declared metadata] --> Score[Deterministic risk scoring 0-100]
  Score --> Label[ready / needs_review / blocked]
  Label --> Dash[Inventory dashboard]
details: Live demo: https://agentmap-fawn.vercel.app. It reports readiness based on what an agent declares about itself; it is not a runtime enforcement layer.

name: approveops
description: Human-in-the-loop approval-workflow prototype for risky agent actions: risk classification, owner-scoped pending queues, and transactional approval/audit writes. The caller must enforce decisions — submitting an action does not itself stop an external agent.
keyFeatures: Keyword-based risk classifier (low / medium / high / critical) — intentionally small and auditable, not a semantic policy engine
Approval lifecycle with genuine transactional guarantees: submission + audit event in one DB transaction, decision + audit event in another
Owner-only and pending-only decision guards with per-user queue isolation
Strongest test suite of the set: transactional persistence, user isolation, and guard behavior covered by mocked-database unit tests
architecture:
graph TD
  Submit[Action submitted] --> Classify[Keyword risk classifier]
  Classify --> Queue[Owner-scoped pending queue]
  Queue --> Decide[Approve / reject - transactional with audit event]
  Decide --> Audit[(Audit log)]
details: Live demo: https://approveops.vercel.app. Database behavior is proven with mocked-database tests; a complete production Clerk/Neon approval lifecycle has not been independently load-tested.

name: agent-cli-mcp-rust
description: A Rust MCP server that dispatches and supervises multiple AI coding CLIs (GitHub Copilot, Google Jules, plus generic executors) over JSON-RPC/stdio.
keyFeatures: Session management for long-running interactive executor runs
Directory isolation: allowedRoots checks block execution outside permitted folders
Destructive-command deny patterns (force pushes, production deploys)
Regex-based secret scrubbing of subprocess output (API keys, JWTs, tokens)
Real Google Jules API integration alongside Copilot CLI dispatch
architecture:
graph TD
  CC[Claude Code] -->|JSON-RPC over stdio| MCP[MCP Transport]
  MCP --> POL[Directory Policy]
  MCP --> RED[Secret Scrubber]
  MCP --> SES[Session Manager]
  SES -->|pipes| Executors[Copilot CLI / Jules / generic]
details: Unit tests cover the policy and redaction modules. The external executor integrations do not yet have end-to-end CI coverage, and no performance benchmarks have been published.

name: antigravity-skill-injector
description: Small proof-of-concept: a Rust JSON-RPC server plus Python scripts that replace full Antigravity skill files with short stubs and serve full skill content on demand, reducing system-prompt size. No test suite and no measured token-savings benchmark.
keyFeatures: Minimal stdio JSON-RPC server serving skill payloads from a JSON registry
Python migration script that parses SKILL.md frontmatter into the registry
Python stub generator plus a one-command bash installer
architecture:
graph LR
  UI[Antigravity UI] -->|renders stubs| Stub[skills/stub.md]
  Agent[Agent context] -->|requests skill| MCP[Rust JSON-RPC server]
  MCP -->|reads| Reg[skills_registry.json]
details: Experiment/proof-of-concept status. Token-savings figures have not been measured, so no percentage claims are made for this project.

name: thesys-c1-dashboard
description: A Next.js dashboard whose panels are restructured live by a Thesys C1 generative-UI agent through a JSON command protocol. Working prototype — CI runs typecheck/lint/build, but there is no test suite and no public demo deployment.
keyFeatures: JSON command protocol parsed from model responses: reorder, add, remove, retitle panels, toggle C1/chat mode
Drag-and-drop dashboard panels with form detection
Six pre-configured panel prompts and an /api/panel endpoint calling Thesys C1
architecture:
graph TD
  Agent[Thesys C1 response] --> Parser[Command-block parser]
  Parser --> Panels[Dashboard panel state]
  Panels --> UI[Drag-and-drop panel grid]
details: Prototype status: no automated tests and no live deployment. The interesting part is the agent-drives-its-own-layout command protocol.

name: About David Papp
description: AI Solutions Developer (contract/freelance) at WEBINFORM IT Ltd since October 2024, and BSc Artificial Intelligence student at Vrije Universiteit Amsterdam (2024-2027 expected). Based in the Rotterdam area, Netherlands. Hungarian citizen with NL/EU work authorization - no sponsorship required.
keyFeatures: At WEBINFORM: builds internal AI tools and production LLM functionality for web applications and ERP-integrated systems - APIs, automation, backend logic, and user-facing AI interfaces
Professional delivery includes 20+ websites/webshops, three internal systems, one user-facing platform, and recovery work on an inherited AI-first service

Repaired an inherited AI-first service with architecture, security, compliance, and prompt/context-injection problems; reverse-engineered undocumented integration behavior and reduced LLM API usage cost by approximately 40%
Two ERP/AI integration projects with direct involvement in discovery, requirements, solution design, client coordination, proposals, and pricing
Previously: Project Assistant at 4iG Nyrt. (Aug 2022 - Aug 2023) and Programming Instructor at Logiscool (Feb-Jun 2023)
architecture:
graph TD
  Work[WEBINFORM - production AI solutions] --> Skills[LLM APIs, ERP integration, backend]
  Uni[VU Amsterdam BSc AI] --> Skills
  Lab[Open-source agent-security prototypes] --> Skills
details: Availability: open to full-time AI engineering, AI solutions, integration, automation, agent infrastructure, and technically oriented product/project roles. Contact: contact@davidpapp.dev. GitHub: https://github.com/pappdavid. LinkedIn: https://www.linkedin.com/in/d%C3%A1vid-papp. Important: the personal open-source projects above are showcase prototypes built outside employment - they are not WEBINFORM products, and none of them are commercial production systems. David has no published fine-tuning of production models; fine-tuning concepts are covered in coursework and personal experimentation only.
